trajectory.description: Observation: Starting fresh, need to build entire pipeline. Analysis: SchNet from PyG is a well-tested equivariant GNN for molecular property prediction. Using 3D coordinates (recommended for best results). Target normalization will help training stability. Decision: Write train.py and predict.py, run 10 epoch baseline with SchNet, submit eval to measure time and get baseline MAE.

Observation: Exit code 139 (segfault) on first run, likely from PyG DataLoader with num_workers>0 or memory issues. The train.py was also truncated in workspace. Analysis: Need to fix the segfault by using num_workers=0, and ensure the full training script is written correctly. Decision: Rewrite both train.py and predict.py with num_workers=0, start with a moderate SchNet model, train for 10 epochs, and submit eval to get baseline.

Decision: Write complete, tested scripts. First verify data loading works, then train. Use a smaller batch size and ensure no truncation.

Analysis: Need to write complete train.py without truncation. The scripts should be complete end-to-end. Decision: Write complete train.py and predict.py, run training for 10 epochs, then submit eval for baseline MAE measurement.

Observation: Training keeps crashing with exit code 139 (segfault). Data loading works (confirmed 105546 molecules), but full train.py segfaults. Analysis: Need to isolate the segfault cause - could be PyG DataLoader, SchNet model, or CUDA issue. Decision: Write a minimal test script to debug if SchNet+PyG works, then based on results either fix the 3D pipeline or fall back to SMILES+fingerprint approach.

trajectory.description: Observation: PyG SchNet approach keeps segfaulting (exit code 139) - likely torch-scatter/sparse compatibility issues with the installed PyTorch. Data loading works but model forward/backward crashes. Analysis: Continuing with PyG is risky without debugging. Switching to SMILES + Morgan fingerprint + MLP approach, which avoids PyG entirely and is very stable. The ceiling is ~0.12-0.14 MAE but a working model beats crashing. Can try 3D approach later if time permits. Decision: Write fingerprint-based train.py and predict.py, train 20 epochs as baseline, submit eval immediately.

Analysis: Need to test torchmd-net (different from PyG, may work) for 3D approach. Also need to submit eval for baseline score. Decision: Submit eval for current model AND test torchmd-net compatibility. If torchmd-net works, switch to 3D training for much better results.

Observation: Fingerprint MLP Val MAE=0.1723, above the 0.12-0.14 SMILES ceiling. Previous SchNet test with 100 molecules worked fine. Early segfaults were likely from truncated scripts. torchmd-net imports fail. Analysis: 3D methods can achieve 0.03-0.06 MAE vs 0.12-0.14 for fingerprints. SchNet with PyG is the best path. Need to try full-scale SchNet training. Decision: Write SchNet training script, unified predict.py, run training. Keep fingerprint checkpoint as backup.

trajectory.description: Observation: SchNet training segfaults with no stdout - crash happens before any output. test_schnet.py worked with 100 molecules. Analysis: Need to identify exactly where crash occurs (import? data load? model? training?) with step-by-step logging. Also prepare a reliable fallback: Morgan FP + Coulomb matrix eigenvalues from 3D data + RDKit descriptors, all without PyG. Decision: Write debug script for SchNet to pinpoint crash, and write hybrid feature-based training as reliable backup. Run debug first, then feature-based training.

Observation: SchNet training keeps segfaulting (exit code 139). test_schnet.py with 100 molecules worked fine. The crash likely happens when creating 105K PyG Data objects at once. Analysis: The segfault is caused by PyG's DataLoader or mass Data object creation with large datasets. Solution: avoid PyG Data/DataLoader entirely - use manual batching by concatenating tensors directly. This is simpler and avoids the problematic code paths. Decision: Rewrite train.py with manual batching (no PyG Data objects, no DataLoader), train 5 epochs to verify it works, then submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: SchNet segfaults (exit 139) consistently, but test_schnet.py with 100 molecules worked. debug_schnet.py crashed at 'from torch_geometric.data import Data, Batch'. My new train.py only imports SchNet (not Data/Batch/DataLoader) and uses manual tensor batching, which may avoid the problematic import path. Analysis: The segfault might be triggered by importing torch_geometric.data.Data/Batch but not torch_geometric.nn.models.SchNet. By only importing SchNet and doing manual batching, I might bypass the crash. Decision: Write compact train.py (SchNet only import + manual batching) and predict.py, run 20 epoch training, submit eval. Backup existing FP checkpoint first.

Observation: PyG's SchNet import causes segfault (exit 139), making 3D GNN via PyG impossible. Current eval MAE is 0.3246 (terrible). Fingerprint approach got ~0.17. Analysis: Need to implement custom SchNet from scratch without PyG dependency, using padded tensor approach for efficient GPU training. This avoids the segfault entirely. Decision: Write model.py (custom SchNet), train.py, and predict.py. Train 30 epochs with the custom model targeting <0.1 MAE.

Need to submit eval immediately to get test score, then consider further training. predict.py appears truncated - must ensure it's complete. Decision: Rewrite complete predict.py, submit eval with current best checkpoint, then start extended training from checkpoint.

Observation: Custom SchNet achieved test MAE=0.0607, within SOTA range. Model was still improving at epoch 50. Analysis: Continued training with fresh LR schedule should push MAE lower. Also, adding EMA (exponential moving average) and LayerNorm could improve generalization. Decision: Modify train.py to resume from best checkpoint, train 100 more epochs with EMA and fresh cosine LR. Submit eval after training completes.

trajectory.description: Observation: Val MAE improved from 0.0606 to 0.0582 (raw model) over 100 more epochs. EMA is hurting (0.0604 vs 0.0582). Previous test MAE was 0.0607. Analysis: Current checkpoint should give better test score. EMA decay too slow, should disable or lower. Model plateaued. Decision: Submit eval with current best checkpoint, then try improvements like larger hidden dim or removing EMA.

Observation: Custom SchNet achieved new best test MAE=0.0581 (improved from 0.0607). Val MAE plateaued at 0.0582 after 150 total epochs. EMA was hurting (0.0604 vs 0.0582 raw). ~50 min remaining. Analysis: Key improvement is to use summed layer outputs (original SchNet design sums atom contributions from ALL interaction blocks, not just the last one). Also remove EMA, use MSE loss, add LR warmup, and try 8 interaction blocks. This is a significant architectural improvement. Decision: Rewrite model.py with summed layer outputs, train from scratch for 150 epochs (~28 min), submit eval.
